data_source: FY1987 annual report data as reflected in Coca-Cola's official historical annual report tables, plus Berkshire 1988/1989 shareholder letters

g4_revenue_trend.description: Net operating revenues rose from about $4.2B (1985) to $4.7B (1986) to $5.4B (1987), with international volume and concentrate economics driving growth before Buffett began buying in 1988.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: Gross profit stayed near 69% of revenue through 1985-1987, showing stable concentrate economics and strong pricing power rather than margin erosion.

g7_moat.description: Coca-Cola had the rare combination of a global consumer brand, a concentrate model with extraordinary economics, and a distribution system embedded in local bottlers worldwide. Consumers asked for Coke by name, retailers had to carry it, and advertising scale reinforced an already dominant habit. Competitors could copy a drink, but not the brand imprint, shelf position, and global bottling footprint.

control_groups.conclusion: BUY — also a strong business, but less pure than Coca-Cola because snacks and restaurant operations diluted the concentrate-style economics Buffett wanted.
PASS — bottling is far more capital intensive and locally competitive than owning the concentrate brand franchise.

buffett_action: Berkshire began buying in 1988; by year-end it owned 14.17M shares costing $592.5M, and by year-end 1989 it had 23.35M shares at a total cost of about $1.02B.
actual_result: One of Berkshire's greatest investments ever — decades of compounding, massive dividends, and a position still held as a core long-term winner.
key_insight: The market saw a familiar soda company; Buffett finally saw a global brand-network system with extraordinary returns, low capital needs at the concentrate level, and decades of runway.
